Moses was doing the ordinary work of a shepherd in the wilderness when the extraordinary interrupted him. A bush burned without burning up—a detail that catches the eye precisely because it violates every expectation of how fire behaves. But notice what moves Moses: not fear, but curiosity. He turns aside to see this marvelous sight. This is important. The God who calls us often does so through the disruption of the ordinary, and our response depends on whether we're paying attention, whether we're willing to pause and look. When Moses steps toward the mystery, God speaks His name. The bush isn't just a miracle; it's an invitation into the presence of the holy. And holiness demands response: take off your sandals, hide your face. You stand on ground that isn't yours.

Yet God doesn't leave Moses cowering in silence. He identifies Himself as the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob—the same God who made promises to Moses' ancestors generations before. This is the God who remembers, who doesn't abandon His people. More than that, God declares that He has *seen* the affliction of Israel in Egypt. He has *heard* their cries. He is *aware* of their sufferings. These aren't abstract acknowledgments. God speaks with the intensity of One who knows His people's pain intimately. The burden is His, not theirs alone anymore. And this God—the One before whom Moses must hide his face—now assigns the burden to Moses: "Go. I am sending you." The calling comes not with the promise that Moses is strong or qualified, but with the promise that God will be with him. This is the exchange God always makes with those He calls: your insufficiency for My presence.

When Moses asks for God's name, he receives not a label but a revelation of God's very being: "I AM WHO I AM." The God who calls Moses is the God who absolutely, necessarily, eternally exists. He needs no explanation, no justification, no origin story. He simply *is*. And what does this God who is Himself *choose* to do? He chooses to come down. He chooses to rescue a slave people. He chooses to work through a frightened shepherd with a stutter. The name Yahweh—the LORD—speaks of a God who is fundamentally free and faithful, who binds Himself to His people not because they deserve it but because He wills it. Everything that follows flows from this: the plagues, the parted sea, the covenant at Sinai—all of it unfolds from the character of the God whose name means absolute presence and unshakeable being.

What strikes hardest is God's honesty about what lies ahead. He tells Moses that Pharaoh won't release the people until a mighty hand compels him. God knows the opposition Moses will face. He doesn't pretend it away or promise an easy victory. Instead, He promises to stretch out His hand Himself, to perform wonders, and to ensure that when Israel leaves, they'll leave enriched—plundered from the very people who enslaved them. God isn't calling Moses to succeed through cleverness or persuasion. He's calling him to witness. Moses' part is to stand before Pharaoh and speak God's word. God's part is to break Pharaoh's will through power that no human can resist. This is the pattern that shapes the entire exodus: God acts, God provides, God delivers—and we are invited to see His hand at work. The question posed to you now is the same one posed to Moses: Will you trust the God who *is*, or will you be paralyzed by what you're not?
